ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general peanut food product class; no specific roasted peanut subclass confirmed. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code refers to peanut as a substance; no specific roasted preparation code confirmed. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical snack serving of roasted peanuts is approximately 30 g (about a small bowl or a handful), providing roughly 175 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Peanuts, all types, dry-roasted, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB No. 16090)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dry-roasted peanuts without salt. This is the closest exact match to plain roasted peanuts as served in a bowl snack context. Sodium value reflects unsalted baseline; lightly salted variants may have higher sodium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: Peanuts (roasted, salted) GI = 14 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). GL per serving calculated as (14 × 6.45 g available carbohydrate per 30 g serving) / 100 = 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Naturally low sodium when unsalted; potassium and magnesium content supports blood pressure regulation
hypertension.guidance: Plain roasted peanuts are low in sodium (6 mg/100 g) and provide potassium (658 mg/100 g) and magnesium (176 mg/100 g), which support healthy blood pressure; however, salted commercial variants can contain 400–600 mg sodium/100 g and should be limited by individuals managing hypertension.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 14) and high fibre and protein content blunt postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of 14 and a glycemic load of approximately 0.9 per 30 g serving, roasted peanuts have a minimal impact on blood glucose and are considered a suitable snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (approximately 30 g per serving).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat content (49.7 g/100 g) but predominantly unsaturated fats; energy-dense food requiring portion control
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Roasted peanuts are rich in monounsaturated (24.4 g/100 g) and polyunsaturated fats (15.6 g/100 g), which are cardioprotective, but their high energy density (585 kcal/100 g) means portion control is essential for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk or body weight.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted peanuts are whole peanuts (Arachis hypogaea) that have been dry-roasted at high heat until golden-brown and crunchy, with or without the addition of salt. They are one of the most widely consumed snack foods across South Asia, Southeast Asia, and globally. In Singapore, roasted peanuts are commonly served as a standalone snack in small bowls at Indian restaurants, hawker stalls, and casual dining establishments. The texture is firm and crunchy with a rich, nutty, slightly toasty flavour. Nutritionally, roasted peanuts are energy-dense (approximately 585 kcal per 100 g), high in protein (23.7 g/100 g), and rich in healthy unsaturated fats including monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids. They are an excellent source of niacin (vitamin B3), folate, vitamin E, magnesium, phosphorus, and potassium. Their very low glycemic index (GI 14) makes them a suitable snack for individuals managing blood glucose levels. The high fibre content (8.5 g/100 g) supports digestive health and satiety. Peanuts are a major allergen and must be clearly declared on menus and packaging. They are naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegetarian, and vegan. Clinically, unsalted roasted peanuts are appropriate for most dietary patterns in moderate portions; salted variants should be used cautiously by individuals with hypertension.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a bowl of roasted peanuts?
Are peanuts good for heart health?
What is the GI of peanuts?
Are roasted peanuts high in protein?
How much sodium in roasted peanuts?
Can I eat peanuts if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dry-roasted peanuts without salt, which is an exact match to the food category. GI value sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication. Both are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2.
notes: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; peanuts contain no industrially produced trans fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; peanuts are a plant food and contain no dietary cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; roasting destroys residual vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; peanuts are a plant food and contain no vitamin B12. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; peanuts do not contain vitamin D. Sodium value (6 mg/100 g) reflects unsalted dry-roasted peanuts; commercially salted variants served at restaurants may contain significantly higher sodium (400–600 mg/100 g). The input cuisine tag 'Chicken Dish' appears to be a data error in the source field survey record and has been disregarded. The food_group has been corrected from 'Mixed' to 'Snacks' as roasted peanuts are a nut-based snack food. GL per serving = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (14 × 6.45) / 100 = 0.9, where available carbohydrate per 30 g serving = (21.5 − 8.5) × 0.30 × 100 / 100 = 3.9 g net carb; using total carbohydrate 21.5 × 0.30 = 6.45 g for GL calculation per standard University of Sydney methodology.
